notes: searches: ~12 (10 shared, 2 stragglers on GC-03-061); 4 WebFetch (productive: TMR 14.04.12 review of Fudge, Tanner's Constance decrees via papalencyclicals.net; failed: Google Books and archive.org fetches for Lenfant 1728 verbatim check). Local scholarship at zero budget cost: Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages vol. 2 (full Constance/Hus safe-conduct treatment, lines 15753-15960); GC 1911 appendix footnotes read directly (fn. 1006 = Lenfant I.516; fn. 1021 = Dowling; fn. 1022 = Mosheim's Dictatus translation + appendix note p. 57 citing Baronius/Gieseler). No Phase-1 defense objects exist for any of the three claims (disputed skipped the defender stage); the verifier rationales were engaged as positions. Anomaly check per charter §7.1: all three as-written rulings are improbable and all three weakened rulings are well-supported (framing gap 3 across the dossier). Re-checked before submission: the dossier deliberately groups three present-tense-1911 continuity claims laid over genuine, accurately cited medieval documents; the medieval fact components all moved FAVORABLE (well-supported), the 1911 continuity layer failed in each — the split is the finding, not verdict drift.
evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. The dossier's shared question is continuity: whether the medieval papacy's documented claims remained the Roman Catholic Church's operative principles and teaching, each claim carrying a present-tense-1911 layer. (1) THE MEDIEVAL DOCUMENTARY CORE IS REAL AND UNDISPUTED. Gregory VII's Dictatus Papae (1075) asserts among its 27 propositions that the pope may depose emperors (prop. 12), may absolve subjects from their fealty to wicked rulers (prop. 27), and that the Roman church has never erred nor will err to all eternity (prop. 22) — the text White's own appendix cites through Mosheim/Gieseler/Baronius, and GC ¶53 quotes it accurately. Innocent III extracted the feudal oath of Peter II of Aragon (1204), described himself in his consecration sermon as 'set between God and man, lower than God but higher than man... who judges all and is judged by no one', and governed by plenitudo potestatis (C. Morris, The Papal Monarchy, Oxford; B. Tierney, The Crisis of Church and State 1050-1300). Boniface VIII's Unam Sanctam (1302) closes with the definition that 'it is absolutely necessary for the salvation of every human creature to be subject to the Roman Pontiff' (text at papalencyclicals.net); Tierney calls it 'probably the most famous' medieval church-state document; the hierocratic theorists (Giles of Rome, De ecclesiastica potestate 1301/02; James of Viterbo; Augustinus Triumphus; Hostiensis on plenitudo potestatis) built the maximal theory around these claims. (2) CONSTANCE AND 'NO FAITH WITH HERETICS'. The facts, from Lea's account (local file, vol. 2) and the conciliar acta: Hus came under Sigismund's unconditional imperial safe-conduct (18 Oct 1414), was imprisoned 28 November; the council's canonists assured Sigismund that 'it could not and might not be in any law that a heretic could enjoy a safe-conduct' (contemporary Constance burgher, in Lea), Sigismund repeating in the 7 June session that 'many say that we cannot, under the law, protect a heretic or one suspect of heresy'; and in session 19 (23 Sept 1415) the council formally decreed that no safe-conduct from any secular power can prejudice the Catholic faith or prevent a competent ecclesiastical judge from trying, judging and condemning a heretic OR SUSPECTED HERETIC, even one who came to judgment relying on the safe-conduct and would not have come without it, the promisor remaining 'in no way obligated' (Lea's rendering, lines 15925-15938; summarized in Tanner's edition of the decrees: 'notwithstanding safe conducts of emperors and kings and others, a competent judge can inquire into heresy'). Lea further documents that the council's self-exculpation (that the safe-conduct postdated the arrest) was 'a lie manufactured for the occasion', contradicted by Sigismund himself in public session. Modern conciliar scholarship converges on the legal substance while rejecting the polemical generalization: Fudge (The Trial of Jan Hus: Medieval Heresy and Criminal Procedure, Oxford UP 2013; via The Medieval Review 14.04.12) finds the trial followed canon-law precedent, safe-conducts being treated as 'irrelevant when it came to accusations of heresy', though the proceedings violated the presumption of innocence; Brandmüller (Das Konzil von Konstanz 1414-1418, 2 vols) likewise judges the trial 'procedurally largely fair' under prevailing law. On the maxim itself, the Catholic-apologetic denial (Pallavicino; Hefele; Catholic Encyclopedia 1908: 'it is not true that the council decreed that no faith should be kept with a heretic') targets the general-perfidy reading — the council issued no canon licensing promise-breaking with heretics generally — but understates the session-19 decree, which did release the promisor in the safe-conduct case. Early modern Catholic opinion was itself split: the inquisitorial canonist Simancas (De catholicis institutionibus) held that faith given to heretics ought not be kept, while the Jesuit Becanus (De fide haereticis servanda, 1607/08; modern academic edition and translation, 2019) argued the duty to keep faith across confessional lines — the position that became and remained mainstream; Trent's 1562 safe-conduct to Protestants was drafted precisely to neutralize the Constance precedent and was honored. No Catholic doctrinal standard of the 1911 era taught the maxim, and the church's accredited organs of that era explicitly denied it. (3) THE CIRCA-1911 STATE OF THE GREGORIAN PRINCIPLES. Two strands separate. Ecclesiological: papal monarchy inside the church stood stronger in 1911 than in the Middle Ages — Vatican I's Pastor Aeternus (1870) defined universal ordinary jurisdiction and papal infallibility (within stated limits: ex cathedra, faith and morals only), and Unam Sanctam's closing definition was still cited as de fide. Political: the deposing power and absolution of subjects were dead as operative claims — Vatican I deliberately defined nothing on temporal power; Bishop Fessler's minimizing reply to Schulte (The True and the False Infallibility) received official approbation from Pius IX; Newman's Letter to the Duke of Norfolk (1875), answering Gladstone's The Vatican Decrees in their Bearing on Civil Allegiance (1874; 150,000 copies sold — the classic statement of White's own thesis by a Protestant statesman), argued the medieval depositions rested on historically conditioned public law; national hierarchies had formally disclaimed the deposing power with Rome's acquiescence (Münster bishops 1774; the British and Irish Catholic oath declarations); no pope of the era asserted it, and 1911-era Catholic reference works treated temporal-jurisdiction ultramontanism as obsolete. Yet in-principle church-over-state doctrine demonstrably persisted: the Syllabus of Errors (1864) condemned the propositions that popes had 'exceeded the limits of their power' (prop. 23), that the church may not employ force or any temporal power (prop. 24), and that church and state ought to be separated (prop. 55); Leo XIII's Immortale Dei (1885) restated the Gelasian dyarchy — two powers, each supreme in its own order — while demanding state recognition of the true religion; Pius X's Vehementer Nos (1906) condemned the French separation law as 'a most pernicious error'; and the confessional-state 'thesis' persisted until Vatican II (J. C. Murray's Leo XIII series, Theological Studies 1952-55, Georgetown Woodstock archive, documents both the persistence and the internal transformation). Whether that persistence amounts to doctrinal continuity or development remains genuinely debated among qualified scholars (Murray and Rhonheimer for development; Thomas Pink for continuity of the coercion doctrine), but no qualified scholar affirms that the Gregorian deposing program functioned as the operative principles of Pius X's church. (4) THE POPE AS MEDIATOR IN MEDIEVAL LAY TEACHING. What laity were actually taught (Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars, Yale UP 1992/2005; the confession-manual literature, Journal of Medieval History 36/4, 2010): direct prayer universally required of every lay person (Pater noster, Ave, Creed), devotion centered on Christ's passion, saints as approachable intercessors, forgiveness through contrition and priestly absolution available from any confessor — a system in which the pope figured as remote juridical head, not as the taught channel of personal access to God; doctrinally Christ alone was the perfect mediator (Aquinas, ST III q. 26), priests and saints mediating ministerially. The vicariate and obedience components, by contrast, are documented: Vicarius Christi standard from Innocent III onward, 'in the place of God' a fair gloss of vicarius Dei usage, Unam Sanctam making subjection to the pope necessary for salvation. SOURCE GENEALOGY: Lea and Lenfant both rest on the Constance acta (the von der Hardt line) but are independent critical treatments; the Catholic Encyclopedia digests Hefele/Pallavicino (one apologetic genealogy, counted once); Fudge and Brandmüller are independent modern specialists on opposite confessional sides converging on the legal substance; the papal instruments (Dictatus Papae, Unam Sanctam, Syllabus, Pastor Aeternus, Immortale Dei, Vehementer Nos) are primary anchors independent of all narrative lines; Murray, Duffy, and Tierney are genealogically independent academic voices.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет три утверждения книги о неизменности папской доктрины: что принципы Григория VII и Иннокентия III остаются принципами Рима в 1911 году, что церковь провозглашает правило «с еретиками не следует держать слова» и что средневековых мирян учили видеть в папе единственного земного посредника на пути к Богу. Средневековое документальное ядро каждого утверждения реально: Dictatus Papae закрепил право низлагать императоров, булла Unam Sanctam объявила подчинение папе необходимым для спасения, а Констанцский собор 23 сентября 1415 года постановил, что охранная грамота не защищает еретика от церковного суда, и это стоило Гусу жизни. В авторских формулировках все три утверждения получили оценку «improbable»: к 1911 году церковь не заявляла право низложения как действующее, её официальные авторы прямо отрицали максиму о еретиках, а мирян учили приступать к Богу через Христа, таинства и святых, но не через папу лично. Ослабленные версии тех же утверждений оцениваются как «well-supported»: собор действительно издал такой декрет и никогда его не отменял, Рим не отрёкся от средневековых притязаний и в 1906 году всё ещё осуждал отделение церкви от государства, а подчинение папе средневековая доктрина объявляла условием спасения. Картину изменили бы документы начала XX века, в которых Рим утверждал бы низложение государей или максиму о еретиках как действующее учение.

quantifier_audit: The principles ... are still the principles (definite, totalizing identity of the whole medieval program with 1911 doctrine)

components.component: Gregory VII and Innocent III asserted the principles the chapter has in view: deposing emperors, absolving subjects from allegiance, supremacy over temporal rulers, ecclesial inerrancy (Dictatus Papae props 12, 22, 27; Innocent III's oath from Peter II of Aragon, 1204).
components.rationale: Primary texts exactly as GC's own appendix cites them (Mosheim tr.; Gieseler; Baronius) and as standard scholarship presents them (Tierney, Crisis of Church and State; Morris, The Papal Monarchy). ¶53's quotations of the Dictatus propositions and the Aragon oath are accurate.

components.component: The ecclesiological strand — papal supremacy over the whole church and infallibility/indefectibility — remained Rome's principle in 1911, indeed strengthened.
components.rationale: Pastor Aeternus (1870) defined universal ordinary jurisdiction and papal infallibility; Unam Sanctam's closing definition was still cited as de fide. In this strand the sentence is simply true, and the ruling records it in the book's favor.

components.component: The political strand foregrounded by ¶53 — the power to depose rulers and absolve subjects from allegiance — remained 'the principles' of the church circa 1911.
components.rationale: Vatican I deliberately defined nothing on temporal power; Fessler's officially approved reply to Schulte minimized exactly these claims; Leo XIII's Immortale Dei framed two powers each supreme in its own order and commanded civic obedience; no pope of the era asserted deposition against any state; national hierarchies formally disclaimed the deposing power with Rome's acquiescence (Münster 1774; British/Irish oath declarations); 1911-era Catholic reference works treated temporal-jurisdiction ultramontanism as obsolete. Non-repudiation and the Syllabus's defense of the medieval popes (prop. 23) sustain a weakened continuity claim, not the identity of the deposing program with 1911 operative doctrine.

components.component: Totalizing identity frame: Rome essentially unchanged, the medieval program still definitive ('the boast of Rome that she never changes' taken at face value in ¶54).
components.rationale: The semper eadem claim the frame leverages covered defined doctrine of faith and morals; the deposing power was never defined doctrine, which is precisely how 1911-era Catholic theology reconciled immutability rhetoric with abandonment of the political program. The frame collapses the distinction the evidence requires: real continuity in ecclesiology and church-state principle, real discontinuity in the operative political claims.

rulings.weakened_version: Rome in 1911 had not repudiated the claims of Gregory VII and Innocent III: its official teaching defended the medieval popes' acts as legitimate (Syllabus prop. 23), still condemned church-state separation (Immortale Dei 1885; Vehementer Nos 1906), and asserted papal supremacy within the church more strongly than ever (Pastor Aeternus 1870).
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the claim as written has three documented layers. (1) Gladstone (The Vatican Decrees in their Bearing on Civil Allegiance, 1874) argued substantially White's thesis: Rome never repudiated the deposing power, and Vatican I converted medieval claims into binding doctrine, compromising Catholics' civil allegiance. It fails on evidence, not confession: the authoritative Catholic responses it provoked — Fessler's officially approbated reply and Newman's Letter to the Duke of Norfolk (1875) — show the church's accredited interpreters denying that the medieval political claims bound anyone, and no subsequent papal act asserted them; the inference from non-repudiation to present operative principle conflates 'unrenounced' with 'in force'. (2) Ultramontanes of the Vatican I generation (Manning, W. G. Ward) held that the power of the keys includes temporal jurisdiction; but their maximal reading lost inside Catholic theology itself, and by 1911 even confessional reference works called it obsolete. (3) Modern continuity scholarship — the intransigentist literature on the 19th-century papacy's medieval-Christendom restoration ideal, and Thomas Pink's argument that the church's coercion doctrine was never repudiated before Vatican II — keeps a genuine continuity question open at the level of in-principle church-state doctrine. The ruling concedes exactly that strand: it is why the weakened version is well-supported and why the claim is improbable rather than discredited. What no qualified scholar affirms is the as-written identity: that the Gregorian deposing program functioned as the operative principles of Pius X's church.

sources.ref: Dictatus Papae (1075), props 12, 22, 27 — Cornell teaching text; English in Mosheim (Murdock tr.) as cited by GC 1911 appendix, p. 57 note
sources.independence_note: Primary anchor; White's own appendix supplies the scholarly chain (Baronius, Gieseler, Mosheim).

sources.ref: Syllabus of Errors (1864), props 19-24, 55; Pastor Aeternus (1870); Leo XIII, Immortale Dei (1885); Pius X, Vehementer Nos (1906) — papalencyclicals.net / vatican.va texts
sources.independence_note: Papal instruments; independent documentary anchors for both the continuity (Syllabus, Vehementer Nos) and discontinuity (Pastor Aeternus's silence on temporal power, Immortale Dei's dyarchy) sides.

sources.ref: J. C. Murray, Leo XIII series (Theological Studies, 1952-55; Georgetown Woodstock Library archive: 'Leo XIII on Church and State', 'Separation of Church and State')
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed; Jesuit confessional location noted; documents both the persistence of the confessional-state thesis to Vatican II and Leo XIII's Gelasian reframing.

sources.ref: W. E. Gladstone, The Vatican Decrees in their Bearing on Civil Allegiance (1874); J. H. Newman, A Letter Addressed to His Grace the Duke of Norfolk (1875)
sources.independence_note: The two poles of the documented 1874-75 controversy on exactly this question; Protestant-liberal and Catholic positions respectively, cited as positions, not arbiters.

sources.ref: Wikipedia, 'Papal deposing power' (Münster bishops' 1774 repudiation; 'obsolete ultramontane idea'); Catholic Encyclopedia (1912), 'Ultramontanism'
sources.independence_note: Encyclopedic corroboration of the abandonment datum; CE additionally serves as primary evidence of circa-1911 Catholic self-understanding; not load-bearing alone under the academic floor.

rulings.would_change_this: A circa-1890s-1900s magisterial act or officially approved theological manual teaching the deposing power as operative doctrine would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open or higher; conversely, a documented pre-1911 formal papal renunciation of the medieval claims would cut the weakened version's non-repudiation core and widen the indictment of the book's rhetoric.
rulings.notes: Date-scoped per the charter note: the ruling concerns 1911, not later developments (Dignitatis Humanae 1965 is outside the claim's tense). The verifier's 'disputed' rationale is sustained in substance and sharpened by decomposition: its two halves (Syllabus continuity vs defunct deposing power) become the well-supported weakened version and the failing as-written component respectively. GC-35-111 ('had she but the power...') is a separate counterfactual claim not ruled here.

quantifier_audit: Faith ought not to be kept with heretics, nor persons suspected of heresy (universal norm, extending to the merely suspect)
she declares (present tense; the church as one continuing declaring subject)

components.component: The quoted words are drawn from the source GC's footnote 1006 names: Lenfant, History of the Council of Constance, I, 516 (ed. 1728).
components.rationale: Verbatim wording could not be independently confirmed this session (Google Books and archive.org fetches failed); the citation is precise (volume, page, edition), the 1911 revision verified quotations, and the substance matches the documented session-19 decree. Lenfant is a Huguenot historian of Constance — a conduit reporting the council, noted as such.

components.component: A conciliar declaration of this substance exists: Constance decreed (session 19, 23 Sept 1415) that no secular safe-conduct protects a heretic or suspected heretic from ecclesiastical trial, judgment and punishment, and that the promisor is released from his pledge; council canonists asserted that no law allows a heretic the benefit of a safe-conduct; the principle governed Hus's case.
components.rationale: The decree stands in the conciliar acta (Tanner's edition, summarized at papalencyclicals.net: 'notwithstanding safe conducts of emperors and kings and others, a competent judge can inquire into heresy'); Lea gives the full text and context, including the council's demonstrably false self-exculpation about the safe-conduct's date; Fudge (Oxford UP 2013) and Brandmüller — independent specialists on opposite confessional sides — confirm the canonical substance ('safe conducts were irrelevant when it came to accusations of heresy'). Note that 'nor persons suspected of heresy' tracks the decree's own extension to suspects: at the 1415 level the quotation is substantively accurate.

components.component: Present-tense attribution: the Roman Catholic Church circa 1911 declares the maxim as her standing doctrine ('she declares', within ¶27-28's 'she is unchanged' frame).
components.rationale: No defined Catholic doctrine ever taught the general maxim; mainstream Catholic theology from Becanus (De fide haereticis servanda, 1607/08) onward taught the duty of keeping faith with heretics, and that line prevailed; Trent's 1562 safe-conduct to Protestants was drafted against the Constance precedent and honored; the church's 1911-era organs (Catholic Encyclopedia 1908) explicitly denied the maxim — whatever the apologetic motive, that is documentary evidence of what the church then declared. The unrepealed session-19 decree is a dormant precedent, not a standing present-tense declaration.

rulings.weakened_version: The Roman church's own general council decreed (Constance, 23 September 1415) that no safe-conduct given to a heretic or suspected heretic binds against ecclesiastical judgment and that the giver of the pledge is released; the decree rationalized Hus's execution under an imperial safe-conduct and was never formally retracted.
rulings.minority_engagement: Both directions engaged. FOR the claim as written: the Protestant-polemical tradition from Lenfant through the 19th-century controversialists, powerfully seconded by Lea's independent verdict that the council operated on the canonical principle that 'the heretic had no rights' and covered itself with 'a lie manufactured for the occasion' — this establishes the medieval substance beyond dispute, but nothing in it establishes a standing 1911 declaration; Lea himself frames the affair as inquisitorial canon law of its period. AGAINST even the weakened version: the Catholic-apologetic denial (Pallavicino; Hefele; Catholic Encyclopedia 1908, 'it is not true that the council decreed that no faith should be kept with a heretic'), which argues the council merely held that a secular power cannot immunize against ecclesiastical jurisdiction. This denial is right against the general-perfidy caricature but understates the actual decree, which extended to suspects and expressly released the promisor — the reason the weakened version stands at well-supported over the apologetic minimum. The intra-Catholic split (Simancas affirming the maxim; Becanus refuting it) shows the maxim was a real position inside early modern Catholicism, never its defined doctrine. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's 'disputed' is resolved by date-scoping: the 1415 declaration is fact, the 1911 present tense is not.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, A History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages, vol. 2 (1888) — local: scholarship/lea-inquisition-middle-ages-vol2-1888.txt, lines 15753-15960 (safe-conduct affair, canonists' maxim, session-19 decree, false self-exculpation)
sources.independence_note: Independent 19th-century archival scholarship, still a standard reference; anti-institutional lean noted; rests on the Constance acta independently of Lenfant's narrative.

sources.ref: Decrees of the Council of Constance, session 19 (23 Sept 1415), Tanner's edition via papalencyclicals.net
sources.independence_note: The conciliar acta themselves; the documentary anchor both polemic and apologetic must interpret.

sources.ref: T. A. Fudge, The Trial of Jan Hus: Medieval Heresy and Criminal Procedure (Oxford UP, 2013), engaged via The Medieval Review 14.04.12
sources.independence_note: Leading modern English-language specialist, non-Catholic, independent of both Lea and the apologetic line; converges with Brandmüller on the canonical substance.

sources.ref: W. Brandmüller, Das Konzil von Konstanz 1414-1418, engaged via Historisches Lexikon Bayerns and konziliengeschichte.org summaries
sources.independence_note: Catholic conciliar historian (confessional location noted); his 'procedurally largely fair' judgment presupposes the same canonical principle the decree states — convergence across confessional lines.

sources.ref: M. Becanus, On the Duty to Keep Faith with Heretics (De fide haereticis servanda, 1607/08; modern academic edition/translation, 2019, academia.edu record); J. Simancas, De catholicis institutionibus (contra)
sources.independence_note: Documents the early modern intra-Catholic debate: the maxim defended by an inquisitorial canonist, refuted by a Jesuit controversialist whose position became mainstream — direct evidence against 'standing doctrine'.
Confessional apologetic digesting Hefele/Pallavicino (one genealogy); used here as primary documentary evidence of what the church's organs declared circa 1911, not as arbiter of the medieval facts.

rulings.would_change_this: An authoritative Roman document of the 1890s-1900s (Holy Office ruling, approved moral-theology manual, catechism) affirming that faith need not be kept with heretics would move the as-written ruling sharply up; a formal pre-1911 papal or conciliar repudiation of the session-19 decree would cut the weakened version's 'never retracted' clause. Verbatim confirmation of Lenfant I.516 would raise component 1 from probable to well-supported without changing the claim-level rulings.
rulings.notes: The extractor's claim_text already hedged ('per the cited source'), but the charter requires ruling on the printed sentence's force: '"..." she declares', present tense, inside ¶28's chameleon/serpent invariance frame. The dual ruling separates the accurate 1415 quotation from the inaccurate 1911 tense.

quantifier_audit: none could approach God except through him
They were taught (generalized medieval lay instruction)

components.component: Medieval laity were generally taught that the pope personally was their earthly mediator, through whom alone they could approach God.
components.rationale: Documented counterexamples defeat the exclusive: every lay person was required to learn and use direct prayer to God (Pater noster, Ave, Creed); devotion centered on Christ's passion, Mary, and the saints as approachable intercessors (Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars); forgiveness was taught through contrition and absolution from any priest (confession-manual literature, JMedHist 2010); doctrinally Christ alone was the perfect mediator (Aquinas, ST III q. 26). The papal-theory steelman (Innocent III 'set between God and man'; Unam Sanctam's subjection-for-salvation definition; the hierocrats) concerns jurisdiction and corporate salvation, not the taught path of personal access to God. Not discredited: the preceding GC sentence extends mediation to 'the priests and prelates to whom he delegated authority', and on that hierarchical reading the sentence compresses real documents; but the printed clause asserts the pope in person ('through him'), and no evidence shows laity taught that.

components.component: They were taught that the pope stood in the place of God (the vicariate).
components.rationale: Vicarius Christi was the standard papal title from Innocent III onward; his consecration sermon claims a place 'between God and man, lower than God but higher than man, who judges all and is judged by no one' (standard text in Tierney and Morris); 'in the place of God' is a fair English gloss of vicarius Dei/Christi as publicly taught and preached. A component ruled in the book's favor.

components.component: They were taught that the pope was to be implicitly obeyed.
components.rationale: Dictatus Papae's obedience propositions; Gregory VII's discipline of rulers; Unam Sanctam's closing definition making subjection to the Roman pontiff necessary for the salvation of every human creature; the excommunication and interdict system enforcing it. Also ruled in the book's favor.

rulings.weakened_version: Medieval teaching made salvation unavailable outside the Roman church headed by the pope — Unam Sanctam declared subjection to the Roman pontiff necessary for the salvation of every human creature — presented the pope as Christ's vicar standing between God and man, and required obedience to him.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest defense of the sentence as written is the hierocratic maximal theory: Giles of Rome's De ecclesiastica potestate and Augustinus Triumphus derived all power, spiritual and temporal, from the pope; Hostiensis' plenitudo potestatis and Unam Sanctam's definition give the pope a soteriologically necessary headship; Innocent III's own 'medius inter Deum et hominem' makes 'earthly mediator' almost a quotation. Add the delegation clause in the preceding GC sentence, and 'through him' can be read as 'through the hierarchy he heads'. The defense fails for the claim as printed on the evidence of what laity were actually taught: high papal theory was curial and academic discourse, not parish catechesis; the documented content of lay religious instruction (universal direct prayer, Christ-and-saints devotion, absolution from any confessor) is incompatible with 'taught that none could approach God except through him [the pope]'. The defense is conceded in full at the weakened level, where the soteriological headship reading is well-supported — the dual ruling records precisely this. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's split ('partly grounded, partly overstated') is sustained and sharpened: the vicariate and obedience components stand, the exclusive-personal-mediation component fails.

sources.ref: E. Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars: Traditional Religion in England 1400-1580 (Yale UP, 1992/2005)
sources.independence_note: Standard revisionist account of late-medieval lay religion; Catholic author writing in a university-press, peer-validated venue — weighed as academic with location noted; independent of the papal-theory literature.

sources.ref: B. Tierney, The Crisis of Church and State 1050-1300 (with documents); C. Morris, The Papal Monarchy (Oxford)
sources.independence_note: Standard scholarly editions/discussions of Dictatus Papae, Innocent III's consecration sermon, and Unam Sanctam; independent of Duffy's devotional-history line.

sources.ref: Innocent III, Sermo in consecratione ('medius inter Deum et hominem, citra Deum sed ultra hominem...'); Boniface VIII, Unam Sanctam (1302), papalencyclicals.net text
sources.independence_note: Primary anchors read through Tierney/Morris; they ground components 2-3 and the weakened version, not the exclusive of component 1.

sources.ref: Aquinas, Summa Theologiae III q. 26 (Christ the one perfect mediator; others mediate ministerially)
sources.independence_note: The doctrinal counterexample to the exclusive, from the church's own normative theology of the period.

sources.ref: 'Lay religion and pastoral care in thirteenth-century England: the evidence of a group of short confession manuals', Journal of Medieval History 36/4 (2010)
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed evidence on what pastoral instruction actually contained; independent of Duffy (different period/corpus).

rulings.would_change_this: Medieval catechetical or sermon evidence (primers, pastoral manuals, model sermons) teaching laity that access to God ran through the pope in person would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open; conversely, evidence that the vicarius Dei title was confined to curial documents and absent from preaching would lower component 2 from well-supported.
rulings.notes: Ch. 3 ¶17 context: the sentence sits between the delegation clause ('priests and prelates to whom he delegated authority') and the punishment claim (GC-03-062, ruled separately in Phase 1); the delegation clause is what makes the weakened hierarchical reading recognizably the same claim rather than a subject change.
